coachNote: Best note yet — you told me your hands are ahead of your memory: "good muscle memory but need more to remember fingerings and which scale is which." So today changes shape. On the scales you own (C, A minor, A harmonic minor) I pulled the fingerings off the page — recall them, and name the key out loud before you play. The ones still settling (the black keys) keep their fingerings. G♭ has sat at the edge three sessions running, so we stop forcing it hands-together and break it into separate hands, slow, to fix the sticky crossing. You called the arpeggio "wayyy too easy," so it graduates to seventh arpeggios, and since chords still feel easy we push them hard: sevenths into a new key (D), sevenths hands-together, and your first 12-bar blues. Rate honestly — and tell me which scale you couldn't name.

exercises.label: C major — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
C D E F | G A B c |]
exercises.cue: No fingerings printed today. Say "C major" out loud, then play it from memory — both hands. If you fumble a finger, that's the point: find it yourself.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if you're truly stuck)

exercises.label: A natural minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F G A |]
exercises.cue: Same white keys as C major — so how do you know it's A minor? It starts and rests on A. Name it, then play it. This is the "which scale is which" muscle.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if stuck)

exercises.label: B♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: Steady at a 4 — fingerings stay printed while it settles. One notch faster again. Watch the 4th-finger landing on the black key.

exercises.label: E♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: Also a 4 — push the tempo. Thumbs find the white keys, the long fingers stay home on the black.

exercises.label: G♭ major — right hand alone (breaking the wall)
exercises.cue: Three sessions stuck at the edge hands-together — so we take it apart. Right hand alone, slow, until the shape is automatic. The thumb falls on C and F (the only white keys).

exercises.label: G♭ major — left hand alone (breaking the wall)
exercises.cue: Now the left hand alone — this is where the hands-together version was breaking. Isolate the thumb-under at C. Get both hands clean separately and next time they'll go together.

exercises.label: A♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Holding at a 4 — keep it crisp. Thumb on C and F, finger 3 starts you.

exercises.label: D♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Second pass at five flats — a touch faster than last time. Thumbs on the two white keys (F and C); everything else is black.

exercises.label: A harmonic minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F ^G A |]
exercises.cue: You owned this hands-together — now from memory, no fingerings. One note makes it "harmonic" instead of natural minor: which one? (The raised 7th, G♯.) Name it, find it, play it.
exercises.fingering: From memory — the only black key is the G♯ near the top

exercises.label: D natural minor — hands together
exercises.cue: Easy one hand yesterday — now together. One flat (B♭). Same finger pattern as C, darker key.

exercises.label: Seventh arpeggios — Dm7 · G7 · Cmaj7
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
"Dm7"D F A c | "G7"G B d f | "Cmaj7"C E G B |]
exercises.cue: You called the plain arpeggio "wayyy too easy" — so here's the upgrade: four-note seventh arpeggios. Roll each chord as a smooth line, hear the colour of that top note.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 2 3 5 up each chord — the top note is the 7th

exercises.label: ii–V–I sevenths in D — Em7 · A7 · Dmaj7 (new key)
exercises.cue: G worked at a 4, so now D — two sharps, F♯ and C♯. Same ii–V–I shape, a new home. Name the three chords before you play them.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 2 3 5 on each — the top note is the 7th

exercises.label: ii–V–I sevenths in C — hands together
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/1
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "Dm7"[DFAc] | "G7"[GBdf] | "Cmaj7"[CEGB] |]
[V:2] D, | G, | C, |]
exercises.cue: The C sevenths are owned one hand — now add the bass. Right hand voices the seventh, left hand names the root. This is how a real ii–V–I sits under your hands.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 2 3 5 · LH single root under each

exercises.label: 12-bar blues in C — your first real form
exercises.cue: Twelve bars, three chords (C7, F7, G7) — the most-played progression in music. The four-chord loops were too easy; this is a real form you can jam over. Learn the order: 4 bars C7, 2 F7, 2 C7, then G7–F7–C7–G7.
exercises.fingering: RH dominant-7th shapes — keep the hand relaxed and let it ring
